EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and winds to the mountains of southeast Idaho with the heaviest amounts in the Upper Snake River Highlands with many road closures including Highway 47, US 20, Highway 32, and Idaho Highway 87.  Many locations recorded over a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system dropped the following snow amounts on SNOTEL sites:  Black Bear 30 inches, Phillips Bench 15 inches, and White Elephant 15 inches.   the following roads were closed by the 17th:  Highway 47, US 20 from Ashton to the Montana border, Highway 32 from Ashton to Tetonia, Highway 87 in Idaho and Highway 33.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and mid elevation snow melt caused the Palouse river to flood on February 4th and February 5th. In the three day period leading up to the flooding a COOP weather station at Potlach recorded 1.16 inches of rain. The river extensively flooded a local road bed but no damage to structures was noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS Palouse River Gage at Potlach recorded a rise above the Flood Stage of 15.0 feet at 8:45 PM February 4th. The river crested at 15.9 feet at 10:45 AM February 5th and then receded below Flood Stage at 10:30 PM February 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level warm front brought snow, difficult travel and tree damage to central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public in Elk City estimated over 5 inches of total snowfall while areas along US-12 between Powell and Lolo Pass saw 8 to 15 inches. During the early morning hours of the 12th, heavy snow changed to rain and caused several trees to snap or become uprooted falling onto US-12 near Lowell. One tree was estimated to be 36 inches in diameter and the Idaho Transportation Department reported that the ground has been saturated and not frozen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system off the Washington coast formed a stationary front draped across eastern Washington and north Idaho during the day of January 11th. A second low pressure formed along this front and migrated through north Idaho. A rich plume of Pacific moisture enhanced into heavy wet snow across the Coeur D'Alene area and the central panhandle mountains. Peripheral areas near the Canadian border and over the Idaho Palouse region and points south received generally 2 to 4 inches of snow from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lookout Pass ski resort reported 23 inches of storm total snow accumulation beginning January 11th and tapering off early in morning of the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of January 23rd a warm front formed over eastern Washington and North Idaho fed by a deep and favorably directed fetch of Pacific moisture. The front became stationary and produced a 36 hour long period of snow impacting the mountains and valleys of far north Idaho with heavy snow accumulations. In the central Idaho Panhandle warm air penetrated the valleys with minor accumulations of wet snow, while the mountains received heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lookout Pass ski area reported 40.0 inches of storm total snow. The nearby Silver Mountain ski area reported 31.0 inches of new snow from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front laying west to east over eastern Washington and north Idaho on February 17th developed into surface low pressure and finally an Arctic cold front which sagged southward through the region. A fetch of Pacific moisture fed into the surface low and enhanced into a period of moderate intensity snow during the day of February 17th and into the morning of the 18th. Heavy snow accumulations resulted over much of north Idaho before very dry Arctic air descended into the region behind the front and ended further precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer at St. Maries recorded 14.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The spring-time freeze and thaw cycle along with melting snow saturating the ground created a debris flow made mostly of rocks from a cliff along Interstate 90 east of Coeur D'Alene near 4th of July Pass on the morning of March 8th. The slide blocked both westbound lanes but one lane was opened up by afternoon. Traffic was reduced to one lane for the next 24 hours while clean up occurred. No automobiles were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho State Police reported that a rock slide closed both westbound lanes of Interstate 90 near 4th of July Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved into north Idaho during the early morning hours of March 24th. While most valley locations received 1 to 3 inches of snow, locally some notably higher amounts were recorded making travel around the region slippery and hazardous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported 4 inches of new snow accumulation at Priest Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of weather disturbances, the first one quickly passing through the region on the evening of February 23rd and through the 24th and the second stronger system transiting the region during the day of February 25th brought heavy mountain snow to the high terrain of the central Idaho panhandle and moderate to locally heavy snow to the Idaho Palouse region, the Coeur D'Alene area and the valleys of the central panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer at Avery reported a two day storm total of 13.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and strong winds occurred for a three day period mainly over the weekend.  The worst conditions were in the Upper Snake Highlands where two feet of snow fell in the Island Park area fell with many road closures.   A dust storm closed interstate 15 north of Idaho Falls on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow combined with strong winds to close the following roads on the 25th:   Highway 32 from Ashton to Tetonia, Highway 87 from Raynolds Pass to the Junction of Highway 20 near Henry's Lake, Highway 20 from Ashton to the Montana border, and interstate 15 from Dubois to the Montana border.   Whiteout conditions were reported with several slide offs.  Snow amounts of 10 inches at Island Park SNOTEL and 25 inches at White elephant SNOTEL were reported.   7 inches was reported by the Dubois COOP observer.   The Island Park COOP observer recorded 26.4 inches and Harriman State Park carried 24 inches.  The Fremont County School District 215 and Teton School District 401 were both closed on the 26th due to the difficult travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow storm which began on February 25th brought periods of heavy snow to Southwestern Idaho.   The heaviest snow fell at elevations above 4000 feet where amounts reached 12 to 18 inches in less than 24 hours.  When the snow ended early in the morning on February 26th,  McCall, Donnelly, and Cascade, Idaho measured 10 to 12 inches of new snow. The higher mountain areas including the ski resorts measured around 18 inches of new snow.  The heavy mountain snow significantly increased avalanche danger during this time which prompted the closure of Idaho 21 until the danger passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 11.5 inches of snow 3 miles NE of Donnelly and 12.0 inches 9 miles SSE of McCall, Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and winds to the mountains of southeast Idaho with the heaviest amounts in the Upper Snake River Highlands with many road closures including Highway 47, US 20, Highway 32, and Idaho Highway 87.  Many locations recorded over a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Howell Canyon SNOTEL recorded 16 inches of snow.   The Magic Mountain SNOTEL recorded 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low tracked northeast across Southwest Idaho producing areas of heavy snow, particularly in the Treasure Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of 5 to 6 inches of snowfall were received around the Boise area. Southeast Boise reported locally heavier amounts of 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late winter storm moved across Idaho spreading heavy snow and strong winds over the West Central and Boise mountains and the Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Cambridge reported 9 inches while the Council Hydromet recorded 10 inches of snow and a trained spotter at Goodrich reported 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day of November 23rd and into the morning of the 24th a strong and moist upper level trough brought a period of accumulating snow to the central Idaho Panhandle. A combination of strong forcing associated with a passing surface low pressure and moist orographic flow into the mountains produced heavy snow in the mountains and 2 to 4 inches of snow over the rising terrain of the Idaho and Washington Palouse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Silver Mountain ski resort reported 10 inches of new snow from this storm system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hazardous travel occurred in Lemhi County during the morning hours of March 1st due to a combination of falling snow and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow across eastern Lemhi County resulted in a period of difficult driving conditions. Wind gust to 40 miles per hour were observed at Gilmore Summit which lofted snow and reduced visibility to near a quarter mile at times. One person who frequents State Hwy 28 described the event as the worst he had ever seen on that stretch of highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely powerful Pacific storm system spread into the central mountains on March 1st and overspread all of southeast Idaho on the 2nd and continued in the far east through the 4th.   Many mountain locations received over 2 feet of snow and even low elevations received 6 to 12 inches from the storm.   Many road closures were reported most notably interstate 15 from Dubois to the Montana border on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The city of Richfield received 7 inches of snow with the Minidoka Schools closing from the storm on March 5th when strong winds after the snow fell made rural roads very dangerous to travel on the morning of the 5th.

Many mountain locations received over 2 feet of snow and even low elevations received 6 to 12 inches from the storm.   Many road closures were reported most notably interstate 15 from Dubois to the Montana border on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow amounts at the following SNOTEL sites:  Bear Canyon 21 inches, Mill Creek Summit 12 inches, Smiley Mountain 30 inches, and Stickney Mill 16 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy snow and strong winds occurred in the Upper Snake Highlands for several days.   Interstate 15 closed from Dubois to the Montana border on the 2nd.  Idaho 32 closed from Ashton to Tetonia and Idaho 47 closed from mile marker 0 to 28.   Heavy snow amounts reported by spotters and COOP observers were:  12 inches in Island Park, 13 inches Driggs, 10 inches Saint Anthony, 14 inches in Small, 13 inches 6 miles west of Kilgore, and 14 inches 4 miles north of Rea.   SNOTEL amounts were 14 inches at Crab Creek and 16 inches at White Elephant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day of November 23rd and into the morning of the 24th a strong and moist upper level trough brought a period of accumulating snow to the central Idaho Panhandle. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of April 7th a strong cold front swept across the Idaho Panhandle. In addition to gradient winds which in general gusted to 30 to 40 mph across the Idaho panhandle, a squall line along the southern part of the front contributed rain cooled downdrafts accelerating winds to locally over 60 mph in Nez Perce County. These winds triggered scattered power outages in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lewiston Airport ASOS station reported a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved through Idaho and very strong winds occurred behind the frontal passage.   Sustained wind speeds over 40 mph were widespread with gusts in the 60 to 70 mph range.   Many road closures due to blowing dust or blowing snow occurred with many trees downed.   Widespread power outages occurred as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of 40 to 60 mph following a cold frontal passage caused extensive power line damage.   Over 400 customers in American Falls, Fort Hall, Chubbuck and Blackfoot were without power on the afternoon of the 2nd.  A large tree was downed in Pocatello as well as one in Blackfoot.   Interstate 86 closed for a short time east of American Falls in both directions due to reduced visibility from blowing dust with multiple collisions.   It was closed from around 5 pm to 845 pm.  A wooden stockade fance blow down along Highway 39 near Moreland.   A tractor trailer was blown over on Highway 39 north of Aberdeen.    Damages were estimated near $150,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved through Idaho and caused significant damage.   Wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph were common throughout the day on the 2nd  especially in the afternoon.  Interstate 15 was closed from exit 118 to exit 143 from Idaho Falls to Roberts due to blowing dust caused from the winds.  A boat was blown off a trailer near Ririe on US Highway 26.   Several trees were reported downed in Idaho Falls including one that fell on top of Holy Rosary Elementary School.  Some structural damage was reported in Idaho Falls due to trees falling on homes.   Damage to power lines resulted in over 2,500 customers without power reported by Rocky Mountain Power.   Power poles were downed in Terreton with nearly 200 customers without power.  Power poles and lines downed in Iona.  A large evergreen tree in Idaho Falls uprooted and took down power lines.  Siding was ripped off one home in Ucon and one home in Ririe.   A shed roof was destroyed in Rexburg.   Damages from the winds was estimated at near $275,000 for the wind storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Photographs and video confirmed a tornado touchdown just north of Darlington at 1:32 pm MDT   The public reported it destroyed wheel lines, uprooted trees, destroyed a chicken coop, pig pen and a shed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage assessment team from the Pocatello National Weather Service Forecast Office traveled to Darlington, Idaho on June 22, 2018. The tornado was believed to have touched down around 1:15pm MDT on June 21, 2018, approximately 5 miles northwest of Darlington, Idaho.  The assessment team found several large hardwood tree limbs down along Huston Road. The largest diameter limb was 1 foot in diameter. About 1.5 miles down Huston Road from Highway 93, a residence on the south side of the road experienced extensive tree damage as well, including a hardwood tree split near the trunk. A second tree was completely uprooted although the root system appeared to be somewhat shallow, perhaps only a couple of feet deep. A third tree was nearly uprooted. A camper was overturned and a large section of the metal roofing had been peeled back. Some of the trusses off of a garage with a metal roof were ripped off. Video of the tornado showed it traveling to the north of Leslie Butte, although it is not clear if it was still on the ground at this point.  ||A second video shows the tornado on the ground approximately 1 mile northeast of Darlington around 1:32pm. The video shows the tornado on the ground for at least 3 minutes while east of Darlington. The damage team found a 40 to 50 foot section of barbed wire fencing down along with several metal fencing posts bent over or ripped out of the ground about 1.5 miles east of Darlington. The tornado lifted around 1:38pm MDT. The National Weather Service received reports of wheel lines, a chicken coop, pig pen, a shed destroyed, and a screen door ripped off. However, the damage assessment team was unable to find this damage. ||Based on the damage, the final assessment for this tornado is a high-end EF0 with winds of 75 to 85 mph. The tornado is estimated to have been 250 feet wide, 7 miles long, and lasted up to 23 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of moderate to heavy rainfall ahead and along a cold front brought flooding and wind damage to portions of central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter saw at least three decidous trees that were knocked down along US-12 east of Kooskia. One tree came down onto a trailer at the River Junction RV Park. Two sheds at the park were blown over by the wind gusts. The spotter heard reports of trees down across US-12, farther up the Lochsa River. A large Ponderosa pine tree was uprooted and fell damaging a fence at a baseball field in East Kooskia. Idaho County Light and Power reported one tree was broken off 30 feet above the ground and they lost a power pole up the Selway River after trees began uprooting and falling over. High winds blew down a rotten tree that took out a section of corrugated roofing of the Glenwood Schoolhouse which was 108 years old. The Syringa RAWS at 3377 feet reported a wind gust of 42 mph that occurred between 1152 and 1252 pm PDT. Grangeville airport reported 47 mph wind gust at 1235 pm PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed over Lemhi County and brought severe hail to North Fork.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail damaged skylights, air conditioning units and caused leaks in a few RV's at a nearby campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms formed over Nez Perce County during the morning hours of May 18th. Lewiston ASOS reported 0.99 inches of rain while the COOP Observer at Nez Perce reported 1.17 inches of rain from these storms. This rain produced a number of secondary road washouts and minor main road damage through out the county. In the Lewiston area urban flooding affected parking lots and poor drainage properties including a number of apartments in Lewiston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five main and 4 secondary roads in Latah County were washed out or otherwise damaged by heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring time snow melt caused the Moyie River to achieve and maintain a minor flood level through the second and third weeks of May. No structures were flooded at this level but fields and unpopulated low lands near the river were inundated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gage on the Moyie River at Eastport recorded a rise above the Flood Stage of 9.0 feet at 9:00 PM PST on May 7th. The river crested at 9.5 feet at 9:30 PM PST on May 8th. The river receded below Flood Stage at 5:45 PM on May 11th.  The river rose again on May 16th and crested at 9.1 feet at 9:15 PM before receding below Flood Stage on May 17th at 7:30 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring time mountain snow melt caused the lake level of Lake Pend Oreille to achieve and sustain a minor flood level from the third week of May through the end of the month. During this event no buildings were flooded however water inundated dock ramps, yards, parkland and parking lots along the lake from Bayview to Sandpoint and flood waters backed up into the Pack River and Clark Fork River channels feeding into the northern portion of  the lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS Lake Level Gage on Lake Pend Oreille near Hope recorded a rise above the Flood Stage of 2063.5 feet on May 18th. The lake level crested at 2063.8 on May 23rd. The lake level remained at or slightly above Flood Stage through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 28th a member of the public photographed a long narrow rope-like funnel cloud near Priest River. It is unknown whether the funnel actually touched down with the lower portion of the distant funnel cloud hidden by trees in the photo. Outreach to local spotters and law enforcement yielded no evidence of damage. Several weak pulse type thunderstorms were noted in the area on radar during the afternoon. No evidence of rotation was detected in any of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The twitter feed of a local TV station featured a photograph from a member of the public appearing to be a funnel cloud near Priest River. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large hail, high winds, tornadoes and heavy rain all occurred in southeast Idaho on the afternoon of the 31st.   Much of it occurred with one supercell which tracked northeast throughout the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed portions of the Idaho National Laboratory (INL) around East and Middle Buttes to the north and east across US 20 (including near the MFC facilities) between 400 and 430 pm MDT.  Due to sparseness of population and terrain, the only damage observed was at mile marker 277 on US Highway 20 at approximately 419 pm MDT.  At this point, large sections of 2 snow fences were heavily damaged  Multiple steel beems were bent and twisted to varying degrees, with portions of those beems that were below the surface and moved approximately one inch.   The horizontal part of these fences, make of vinyl and steel cable, were ripped apart and strewn for several yards.  Large metal pieces used to hold these together were snapped and/or thrown several yards away from the fence.   Other indications of tornado damage included a sign snapped off at the ground, sagebrush that was partially or completely ripped from the ground, and several small size lava rocks tossed an unknown distance.  ||Based on that evidence, the tornado was assigned an EF-1 rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Seasonal snow melt flooding along with heavy rain caused significant damage to the levee near Lorenzo  as it was eroding 500 feet per day.    The Snake River and Henry's Fork of the Snake flowed at action stage for several weeks causing the event.   Jefferson County declared disaster as it was costing 9,000 dollars per day for seven days.  The County used all of their 40,000 dollars for the repairs to the levee and required further assistance from the US Corps of Engineers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms formed during the evening of May 20th over the Palouse region of Washington and Idaho.  Heavy rain in the cores of these storms produced local flash flooding and debris flows in largely uninhabited rural areas but temporarily cut   some highways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An above average snow pack in the mountains drained by the Pend Oreille and Clark Fork River basins melted at an accelerated rate during the month of May. Run off from this snow melt prompted the Albeni Falls Dam to open the floodgates and bring the Pend Oreille River in Idaho and Washington to moderate flood level for an extended period of time from the middle of May through the first week of June.  Only a short The portion of the river exists in Idaho below the dam and no damage was reported in Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Flow Gage at Abeni Falls Dam which controls the flow of the Pend Oreille River below the dam surpassed the Flood Flow of 95,000 Cubic Feet per Second (CFS) at 2:30 PM PST on May 11th. The flow increased and passed through the Moderate Flood Flow of 115,000 CFS at 12:15 PM on May 18th. The flow continued to increase and peaked at the Moderate Flood reading of 121,000 CFS at at 5:00 AM on May 27th. The flow continued to fluctuate between 118,000 CFS and 121,000 CFS through the end of May. The flow receded to below the Flood Flow on June 6th.||Above Albeni Falls Dam the river is a slow moving extension of Lake Pend Oreille. The USGS Lake Level Gage on Lake Pend Oreille near Hope recorded a rise above the Flood Stage of 2063.5 feet on May 18th. The lake level crested at 2063.8 on May 23rd. The lake level remained at or slightly above Flood Stage through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought a record breaking heat wave across lower elevations in north central Idaho August 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pittsburg RAWS reached 116 on the 9th and 119 on the 10th.  RAWS have a well documented warm bias, so these temperatures are difficult to compare to other observations. Nevertheless, in this notoriously hot location, 119 appears to be the warmest in its period of record going back to 1993.  Furthermore, the Slate Creek RAWS reached 113 on the 9th and 114 on the 10th. Finally, Riggins reached 109 on the 10th, one degree shy of the record high of 110, with observations going back to 1896.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the month of August numerous large wildfires in north Idaho, northeast Washington, Montana and British Columbia contributed to periods of Unhealthy air quality with a few readings in the Hazardous category in North Idaho. The Northern Idaho Panhandle with several large wildfires burning nearby suffered the worst and most persistent air quality issues, while more southerly locations more distant from active fires only occasionally witnessed severely degraded air quality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The air quality sensor in Lewiston recorded a period of Unhealthy to Very Unhealthy air quality on August 21st due to smoke from regional wildfires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the month of August numerous large wildfires in north Idaho, northeast Washington, Montana and British Columbia contributed to periods of Unhealthy air quality with a few readings in the Hazardous category in North Idaho. The Northern Idaho Panhandle with several large wildfires burning nearby suffered the worst and most persistent air quality issues, while more southerly locations more distant from active fires only occasionally witnessed severely degraded air quality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Air quality Sensors at Sandpoint and near Bonners Ferry measured periods of Unhealthy air quality during the third week of August due to smoke from nearby wildfires. Air quality deteriorated into the Hazardous range at both locations on August 19th and 20th, and remained occasionally Unhealthy until a cold front passage with significant rain passed through the region on August 27th.

The Northern Idaho Panhandle with several large wildfires burning nearby suffered the worst and most persistent air quality issues, while more southerly locations more distant from active fires only occasionally witnessed severely degraded air quality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An air quality sensor near Hayden recorded periods of Unhealthy air quality during the third week of August as smoke from regional wildfires drifted into the area. Air quality  deteriorated into the Very Unhealthy range for much of August 20th and into the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought a record breaking heat wave across lower elevations in north central Idaho August 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pierce set a record high of 101 on August 9th and an all-time record high of 105 on August 10th.  The two day mean maximum temperature of 103 at Pierce was the hottest on record with observations going back to 1963.  Elk River reached a high of 98 on the 9th and then set a record high of 102 on August 10th. Even though RAWS sites have displayed a warm bias, the Dent RAWS in close proximity to the Dworswak Reservoir reached 106 on the 9th and 105 on August 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought a record breaking heat wave across lower elevations in north central Idaho August 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cottonwood set record highs of 103 on the 10th and 99 on the 9th. The two day mean maximum temperature of 102 is the hottest two day stretch since observations began in 1976. Grangeville reached 104 on the 10th and 99 on the 9th. Kamiah just inside Spokane's County Warning Area recorded the second hottest two day mean maximum temperature stretch with 109.5. Kamiah set record highs of 109 on the 9th and 110 on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Sharps fire began by human cause on July 29th via exploding targets.   It continued through a good part of August and grew to 64,853 acres and located approximately 6 miles east of Bellevue on Sharps Canyon Road. The BLM closed public lands in and around the Sharps fire in Blaine County. This included all BLM-managed lands east of Highway 75, south of East Fork Road, then south of the Sawtooth National Forest Boundary, west of Fish Creek Road, and north of US Highway 20, and east of Gannett Road.   Other closures were the following:  Muldoon Canyon Road is closed from EE-DA-HO Ranch (east of Bellevue). East Fork Road is closed at Triumph. Little Wood Reservoir Road is closed at the Hunt Lane. Quigley, Slaughterhouse Creek Roads, Indian Creek, and Ohio Gulch Roads are closed where the pavement ends. High Five, Road Canyon Road and Baugh Creek, are also closed.   No structures were damaged.  Mandatory evacuations were ordered for Little Wood recreation Area campground and all residences on Little Wood Reservoir Road and Flat Top Road north of the reservoir.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sharps fire began by human cause on July 29th via exploding targets.   It continued through a good part of August and grew to 64,853 acres and located approximately 6 miles east of Bellevue on Sharps Canyon Road. The BLM closed public lands in and around the Sharps fire in Blaine County. This included all BLM-managed lands east of Highway 75, south of East Fork Road, then south of the Sawtooth National Forest Boundary, west of Fish Creek Road, and north of US Highway 20, and east of Gannett Road.   Other closures were the following:  Muldoon Canyon Road is closed from EE-DA-HO Ranch (east of Bellevue). East Fork Road is closed at Triumph. Little Wood Reservoir Road is closed at the Hunt Lane. Quigley, Slaughterhouse Creek Roads, Indian Creek, and Ohio Gulch Roads are closed where the pavement ends. High Five, Road Canyon Road and Baugh Creek, are also closed.   No structures were damaged.  Mandatory evacuations were ordered for Little Wood recreation Area campground and all residences on Little Wood Reservoir Road and Flat Top Road north of the reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions over north Idaho during July and August created dry fuel conditions across the region. Lightning from occasional thunderstorms moving through the region most notably during the last week of July, caused a number of wildfires some of which grew large and long lasting requiring considerable state and federal resources to contain. These large fires were in unpopulated forest land with no structures or homes lost. Smoke from these fires and fires in surrounding states and provinces often severely degraded air quality across eastern Washington and north Idaho during the month of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cougar Wildfire near Hope Idaho is believed to have been started by lightning on July 28th. The fire grew and eventually burned 7,866 acres of timber. No structures of homes were lost or threatened. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at 5.1 million dollars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Surprise Creek Wildfire in a remote portion of Shoshone County is believed to have been caused by lightning on July 27th. It grew and eventually burned 3300 acres of timber in a wilderness area. No structures were threatened or lost. The cost of firefighting resources to contain the blaze was calculated at 1.3 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions over north Idaho during July and August created dry fuel conditions across the region. Lightning from occasional thunderstorms moving through the region most notably during the last week of July, caused a number of wildfires some of which grew large and long lasting requiring considerable state and federal resources to contain. These large fires were in unpopulated forest land with no structures or homes lost. Smoke from these fires and fires in surrounding states and provinces often severely degraded air quality across eastern Washington and north Idaho during the month of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rampike Wildfire is believed to have been caused by lightning on July 31st. The fire burned 3100 acres in a remote wilderness area of Shoshone County. No structures were lost or threatened. The cost of the resources necessary to contain the fire was calculated at  1.0 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An energetic vorticity maximum moved through the deep trough situated over the Pacific Northwest producing convective, heavy snow showers across the Treasure Valley of Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to ten inches of new snow fell mainly from Boise to Mountain Home. Reports ranged from four inches at the National Weather Service to seven inches in Southeast Boise and ten inches in Mountain Home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of June 9th scattered convective showers developed over north Idaho. A weak and short lived funnel cloud formed near Rathdrum. Radar indicated some minor convective showers in the area but no rotational signatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public photographed a weak funnel cloud near Rathdrum. The funnel cloud quickly dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Big Lost Wood River flooded at Leslie on Highway 93.  Two homes were affected at the foundation with flooding.   They were upstream of the Highway 93 bridge over the Big Lost River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Lost Wood River flooded at Leslie on Highway 93.  Two homes were affected at the foundation with flooding.   They were upstream of the Highway 93 bridge over the Big Lost River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 500 acre wildfire occurred over Massacre Rocks about 5 to 10 miles southeast of American Falls. on June 9th and 10th.   The fire was driven by wind gusts of 40 mph and spread rapidly.   Cooler temperatures and lighter winds slowed growth and it was contained quickly over open land.   No structures were threatened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 500 acre wildfire occurred over Massacre Rocks about 5 to 10 miles southeast of American Falls. on June 9th and 10th.   The fire was driven by wind gusts of 40 mph and spread rapidly.   Cooler temperatures and lighter winds slowed growth and it was contained quickly over open land.   No structures were threatened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the Big Wood River for several months occurred in the Della View neighborhood of Hailey.   It finally ended in June but the total cost of the extensive flooding period resulted in $750,000 of insurance claims in the neighborhood and $200,000 for flood response and public property cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the Big Wood River for several months occurred in the Della View neighborhood of Hailey.   It finally ended in June but the total cost of the extensive flooding period resulted in $750,000 of insurance claims in the neighborhood and $200,000 for flood response and public property cleanup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began near American Falls on June 14th and quickly grew to 800 acres.   It was contained on June 15th.  No structures were threatened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began near American Falls on June 14th and quickly grew to 800 acres.   It was contained on June 15th.  No structures were threatened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from a thunderstorm brought neighborhood street flooding to Idaho Falls with ankle deep water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from a thunderstorm brought neighborhood street flooding to Idaho Falls with ankle deep water.  The main flooded area was the Stonebrook subdivision off of Sunnyside Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the night of February 13th a surface low pressure moved through eastern Washington. North Idaho was under the cold side of this low pressure track and received widespread heavy snow during the day on February 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Scweitzer Mountain ski resort 12.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow amounts fell above 7 thousand feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vienna Mine SNOTEL recorded 15 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow amounts fell above 7 thousand feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow amounts at high elevations SNOTEL sites were:  10 inches at Dollarhide Summit, 9 inches at Galena, 11 inches at Galena Summit, 6 inches at Hyndman, and 10 inches at Lost Wood Divide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A total of 3 to 6 inches of snow fell in the Pocatello and Chubbuck area on Christmas day causing travel problems with extensive accidents on interstates 15 and 86 mainly on Christmas day evening after snow that fell re-froze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow and wind brought travel problems with localized heavy snow along with road closures and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow combined with wind and traffic caused 16,000 customers to be without power by the morning of December 31st.   Saint Anthony recorded the heaviest snowfall with 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow and wind brought travel problems with localized heavy snow along with road closures and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow and strong winds closed US Highway 30 from Lava Hot Springs east to Soda Springs on the 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway 34 was closed between milepost 63 near Panting Lane and the Wyoming State line closed due to snow and wind with blowing snow.  Swan Valley recorded 6.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow and wind brought travel problems with localized heavy snow along with road closures and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind and heavy snow caused some Fremont customers to be without power on the 30th.  The Island Park COOP observer recorded 12.3 inches of snow on the last 3 days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and wind caused schools to shut down early in Minidoka County on December 12th at 1:30 PM MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and wind caused schools to shut down early in Minidoka County on December 12th at 1:30 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist warm front developed over north Idaho during the day of December 11th followed by a cold front during the evening. The valleys of far north Idaho received heavy wet snow accumulations which turned over to rain late in the day. South of Sandpoint valleys received generally light to moderate amounts of wet snow which turned to rain in the afternoon. The mountains of the entire north Idaho panhandle received all snow which accumulated to heavy amounts especially after the trailing cold front passage during the evening of December 11th when winds became favorable for persistent upslope snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Scweitzer Mountain ski resort reported 11.0 inches of new snow from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two to three feet of snow fell at the Pebble Creek ski area the first couple of days of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bonneville Peak and the Pebble Creek ski area received 2 to 3 feet of snow through December 3rd.  The ski area reported 2 feet at the base and 3 feet at the top of the main lift.  Ten inches fell in the town of Inkom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist warm front passed through the region on December 28th. Widespread moderate and locally heavy snow amounts were noted in the mountains and valleys of north Idaho. During the late afternoon and evening of December 29th the following cold front passed through the region. A deep fetch of Pacific moisture along this front fell mainly as rain in the valleys but in the mountains of the Idaho Panhandle heavy snow was noted and impacted travel across area passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple CoCoRaHS observers reported 3 to 4 inches of snow accumulation in the valleys of the northern Idaho Panhandle with an overnight warm front passage. The snow tapered off to rain showers during the morning of December 28th with no further accumulation in the valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern hills and Albion Mountains were hit with a significant fall snow storm.   The snow fell on October 9th and 10th and 32 hour totals were 13 inches at Pomerelle Ski Resort, 8 inches at Bostetter Ranger Station and 12 inches at Howell Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern hills and Albion Mountains were hit with a significant fall snow storm. The snow fell on October 9th and 10th and 32 hour totals were 13 inches at Pomerelle Ski Resort at the top of the mountain and 8 inches at the base, 8 inches at Bostetter Ranger Station and 12 inches at Howell Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow fell across North-central Idaho into western Montana, impacting both valleys and mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snow lead to difficult travel during the morning hours. Snowfall of 4 to 8 inches were commonly observed by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow fell across North-central Idaho into western Montana, impacting both valleys and mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snow lead to difficult travel during the morning hours. Snowfall of 5 to 10 inches were commonly observed by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow impacted higher elevations of western Montana and north central Idaho, including mountain pass areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snowfall created impacts to travel over Lolo Pass on Highway 12, with up to 14 inches of snowfall being reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow impacted higher elevations of western Montana and north central Idaho, including mountain pass areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snowfall led to 12 inches of snow in Pierce and generally 7 to 14 inches elsewhere. Areas of blowing snow were also an issue on Highway 11 near Greer Grade.
